perturbation.original_text: Marketing Affiliate shall give Equidata written demand of dispute within 10 days of the due date of the invoice. The demand shall set forth a statement for the nature of the dispute and the amount involved. If Equidata and Marketing Affiliate can not resolve the dispute on their own within 10 days after Equidata receive said dispute, the parties shall jointly select an arbitrator.
perturbation.changed_text: Marketing Affiliate shall give Equidata written demand of dispute within 5 days of the due date of the invoice. The demand shall set forth a statement for the nature of the dispute and the amount involved. If Equidata and Marketing Affiliate can not resolve the dispute on their own within 2 days after Equidata receive said dispute, the parties shall jointly select an arbitrator.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the dispute resolution timeframe could be problematic under general contract law principles requiring a reasonable opportunity to resolve disputes, and potentially conflict with industry-specific dispute resolution norms that could allow a more extended timeframe. Disputes regarding accuracy of credit reports, which is related to the services provided, has specified dispute timeframes as outlined by the FCRA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the dispute window to 5 days, and the resolution period to 2 days, for what are likely credit report disputes, potentially violates the reasonable investigation requirement as outlined in 15 U.S.C. § 1681i. This federal statute specifies the duties of furnishers of information upon notice of dispute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: L. 104–208, §2409(a), inserted heading and amended text of subsec. (a) generally. Prior to amendment, text read as follows: "If the completeness or accuracy of any item of information contained in his file is disputed by a consumer, and such dispute is directly conveyed to the consumer reporting agency by the consumer, the consumer reporting agency shall within a reasonable period of time reinvestigate and record the current status of that information unless it has reasonable grounds to believe that the dispute by the consumer is frivolous or irrelevant. If after such reinvestigation such information is found to be inaccurate or can no longer be verified, the consumer reporting agency shall promptly delete such information. The presence of contradictory information in the consumer's file does not in and of itself constitute reasonable grounds for believing the dispute is frivolous or irrelevant." Subsec. (d). Pub. L. 104–208, §2409(b), struck out at end "The consumer reporting agency shall clearly and conspicuously disclose to the consumer his rights to make such a request. Such disclosure shall be made at or prior to the time the information is deleted or the consumer's statement regarding the disputed information is received." L. 108–159, title III, §313(b), Dec. 4, 2003, 117 Stat. 1994 , provided that: "(1) Study required .-The Board and the Commission shall jointly study the extent to which, and the manner in which, consumer reporting agencies and furnishers of consumer information to consumer reporting agencies are complying with the procedures, time lines, and requirements under the Fair Credit Reporting Act [this subchapter] for the prompt investigation of the disputed accuracy of any consumer information, the completeness of the information provided to consumer reporting agencies, and the prompt correction or deletion, in accordance with such Act, of any inaccurate or incomplete information or information that cannot be verified. "(2) Report required .-Before the end of the 12-month period beginning on the date of enactment of this Act [Dec. 4, 2003], the Board and the Commission shall jointly submit a progress report to the Congress on the results of the study required under paragraph (1). "(3) Considerations .-In preparing the report required under paragraph (2), the Board and the Commission shall consider information relating to complaints compiled by the Commission under section 611(e) of the Fair Credit Reporting A
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the timeframe for dispute resolution, potentially violating the 'reasonable period of time' requirement for reinvestigation as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 1 regarding disputes conveyed to consumer reporting agencies. The original contract allowed 10 days for both dispute demand and resolution, while the changed text reduces it to 5 and 2 days, respectively, which could be deemed unreasonable under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, especially concerning credit report disputes.

perturbation.original_text: Notice will be given to Marketing Affiliate in writing no less than 30 days prior to such increase taking affect.
perturbation.changed_text: Notice will be given to Marketing Affiliate in writing no less than 10 days prior to such increase taking affect.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for price increases to 10 days might be considered an unreasonable period, depending on industry standards, and may conflict with general principles of good faith and fair dealing in contracts. This also potentially can interfere with the Marketing Affiliate's ability to give the customer proper notification as to the credit increase.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 12 CFR § 1026.9(c) specifically relates to changes in credit card terms, it establishes a benchmark for providing reasonable notice of changes to terms and conditions. Applying this principle analogously, a 10-day notice period for price increases might be deemed insufficient, especially if it impacts consumer pricing. This is particularly important in this agreement, since this relates to credit reporting, and can impact the end user / consumer.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 days to 10 days for notification of price increases creates a potential inconsistency regarding reasonable notice, which can lead to legal contradictions. The Law Explanation references 12 CFR § 1026.9(c) establishing a benchmark for providing reasonable notice. The original text provided a more reasonable timeframe, while the changed text may be deemed insufficient. The Perturbation Explanation states this reduction may conflict with general principles of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
